data.title: What may happen in the aftermath of the Los Angeles fires
data.description:
By Philip van Doorn

Also: The quantum-computing-stock meltdown, surging bond yields and how to decide whether or not a Roth retirement account is best for you

Several wildfires in Los Angeles Country were still burning on Friday, with at least 10 people killed and 350,000 evacuated, according to a Wall Street Journal report. AccuWeather estimated on Thursday that the total economic fallout from the fires would range from $135 billion to $150 billion.

During months leading up to the L.A. fires, some insurers were already trying to cut their risk of losses from wildfires by refusing to renew homeowner's insurance policies in certain areas of California. The severity of the damage may lead to action by government agencies to address the state's property-insurance crisis. Insurers are regulated by the states. Mortgage lenders and loan servicers are required by state and federal regulators to make sure collateral properties have sufficient insurance coverage.

But before any government action can take place to mitigate the cost of wildfire insurance coverage, the heaviest insurance losses are likely to be borne by these three companies.

The displacement of so many people in the Los Angeles area might reverse the area's recent decline in rents. Rents are likely to increase in the wake of the wildfires.

More: California wildfires send power company Edison International's stock falling

How to cause a stock-market niche to plunge

The above chart shows a remarkable upswing for shares of four small-cap (or microcap) stocks of companies developing quantum-computing technology. Through Wednesday, all four stocks showed incredible gains from three months earlier. (The U.S. stock market was closed on Thursday, when a state funeral was held to honor former President Jimmy Carter.)

But even though the stocks were way up through Wednesday, they had reversed during that trading session, with declines of 45% for Rigetti Computing Inc. (RGTI), 43% for Quantum Computing Inc. (QUBT), 39% for IonQ Inc. (IONQ) and a 36% drop for D-Wave Quantum Inc. (QBTS).

The quantum group's thunder was stolen - at least briefly - by Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) chief executive Jensen Huang. When speaking with analysts on Tuesday at the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas, Huang said an estimate that it would take 15 years to develop "simple" quantum computers "would probably be on the early side."

More coverage:

  • Wall Street's riskiest stocks are melting down as retail traders face 'day of reckoning'
  • Jensen Huang crushed the quantum stocks, and now Martin Shkreli is going for the kill

A bond-market transition

Early on Friday, 10-year U.S. Treasury notes BX:TMUBMUSD10Y were trading at a yield of 4.74%, which was up five basis points from the previous session and up from 3.79% at the end of September.

Joy Wiltermuth described the economic and political factors behind the recent spike in Treasury yields and how investors might be looking at an excellent buying opportunity.

More bond-market coverage:

  • Are 'Magnificent Seven' bonds now worth buying after months-long selloff?
  • Paul Krugman thinks bond yields may be rising due to an 'insanity premium'
  • Tech stocks got slammed because economic data sparked a jump in bond yields

Related: Why investors are still buying gold despite a strong dollar and rising Treasury yields

Meta MAGA

Social-media companies have long faced pressure to moderate content, to limit the spread of false information or hate speech. But this week Meta Platforms Inc. (META) Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg announced the company would make changes to "get back to our roots around free expression," and that he would "work with President Trump to push back on governments around the world that are going after American companies and pushing to censor more."

Full coverage:

  • Meta goes 'MAGA': Facebook adjusts fact-checking approach as Zuckerberg touts 'free expression' and cites 'cultural tipping point'
  • 'Companies always cozy up to whoever is in power': Why Meta is numb to outrage over its fact-checking changes

Related: TikTok ban goes before Supreme Court. Here's where the app's $10 billion in ad revenue could go if it dies.

To Roth, or not to Roth?

For U.S. savers and investors, a traditional individual retirement account (IRA), 401(k) or 403(b) employer-sponsored retirement account is funded with pretax dollars. That means contributions made during a year will not be part of that year's taxable income. Then any interest and dividends earned in the account over the years will not be taxed. But once the account holder is eligible to withdraw money without a penalty, any withdrawals will be taxed as regular income.

On the other hand, a Roth IRA, Roth 401(k) or Roth 403(b) account is funded with after-tax dollars. This means the account holder pays taxes on earned income contributed to a Roth account during the year - but that's it. None of the earnings within the account will ever be taxed and any withdrawals (once the account holder reaches the eligible age) will never be taxed either.

Do you think it is simple to decide between a traditional retirement account or a Roth account? Despite strong opinions among some financial advisers who favor one approach or the other for all of their clients, the devil is in the details. Brett Arends digs into new research that can help you decide between a Roth account and a regular retirement account.

Nvidia helps Micron

During a speech at the Consumer Electronics Show on Tuesday, Nvidia chief executive Jensen Huang mentioned that Micron Technology Inc. (MU) was providing memory for Nvidia's new GeForce RTX 50 Blackwell family of graphics processing units for PC gaming. Through Thursday, Micron's stock was up 14% from a week earlier.

Here is what name-drops from Nvidia can mean for other companies.

More Big Tech coverage:

  • Micron's stock leads the S&P 500 for 2025. Here's why more gains could follow.
  • AMD is going head-to-head with Nvidia in AI-backed drug discovery with new deal
  • Nvidia's stock falls further - but analysts see a future that's brighter than ever

The 'Fix My Portfolio' column covers various money matters

This week in the Fix My Portfolio column, Beth Pinsker helped a MarketWatch reader who described a difficult problem with her brother "squatting" in a house that is owned by their mother, who lives elsewhere. The brother refuses to talk to other family members.

On a completely different note, a reader asked for help managing the Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amounts (IRMAA) that may increase his Medicare premiums. He needs to plan two years ahead.

Medical bills may no longer affect your credit score - should this affect your decision on whether or not to pay?

On Tuesday, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau finalized a new credit reporting rule, to "ban the inclusion of medical bills on credit reports used by lenders and prohibit lenders from using medical information in their lending decisions."

Has fear of a declining credit score affected your decisions when disputing medical bills? Andrew Keshner dug into the new rule, how credit scoring works and how the new rule might affect you.

Stock lists

In the wake of Kratos Defense & Security Solutions Inc. (KTOS) landing a large government contract for hypersonic missile testing, James Rogers named other companies that can benefit from this weapons development trend.

Stock screens:

  • Most of the 'Sweet 16' stocks have dominated. They might do it again in 2025.
  • 10 stocks making best use of investors' money. Here's who beats Nvidia.

Gambling is risky, even for the house

Here is how Flutter Entertainment PLC (FLUT), the owner of FanDuel, took big losses as customers placed winning bets on NFL games.

-Philip van Doorn

data.title: Walgreens CEO Says Renegotiated Contracts Are Helping Turnaround Efforts — Market Talk
data.description: Walgreens Boots Alliance's contract negotiations are going well. CEO Tim Wentworth on a call with analysts says the drugstore chain has gotten "pretty darn close" to the contracts it had hoped to achieve with its pharmacy benefit managers. The company has created a category for higher-cost drugs, such as GLP-1s, and rebalanced its mix of brands and generics to more appropriately reflect the environment today, resulting in cost savings. "We still have a ton to do," Wentworth adds, noting that only one-third of the company's contracts have been renegotiated at this time. Shares jump 25% after Walgreens beats estimates in F1Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1224 ET - Walgreens Boots Alliance's contract negotiations are going well. CEO Tim Wentworth on a call with analysts says the drugstore chain has gotten "pretty darn close" to the contracts it had hoped to achieve with its pharmacy benefit managers. The company has created a category for higher-cost drugs, such as GLP-1s, and rebalanced its mix of brands and generics to more appropriately reflect the environment today, resulting in cost savings. "We still have a ton to do," Wentworth adds, noting that only one-third of the company's contracts have been renegotiated at this time. Shares jump 25% after Walgreens beats estimates in F1Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1145 ET - Constellation Brands' consumers are ditching the company's cheaper wine options, CEO Bill Newlands says on a call with analysts. The company saw a 16% decline in wine and spirits shipments in the fiscal third quarter, which in turn was the primary driver of the respective 14% and 25% declines in net sales and operating income for that business, the executive says. Meanwhile, the company's fine wine portfolio saw depletion growth of 6% in its largest premium wine brands. Overall, the company is focused on going through cost savings and operational efficiency initiatives to drive leverage to the bottom line. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1144 ET - Modelo Especial grew depletions by over 3% in its fiscal third quarter and upheld its position as the top share gainer in U.S. track channels, Constellation Brands' CEO Bill Newlands says on a call with analysts. Corona Extra depletions, on the other hand, declined about 1%, but continued to increase its dollar share as a top ten gainer in the category, Newlands says. "We remain positive about the outlook for Corona Extras growth, as it continues to be the most beloved beer brand in the U.S., and has responded well to the incremental marketing spend deployed in the third quarter," Newlands says. Pacifico delivered another quarter of "very strong" depletion growth of nearly 20%, the executive adds. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1130 ET - Constellation Brands' marketing efforts put in place for its beer business in the second quarter to offset a softer macroeconomic backdrop that began affecting consumer spending during this summer bear fruit in the third quarter, CEO Bill Newlands says on a call with analysts. Still, beverage alcohol remains relatively unchanged in its share of total consumer expenditures, Newlands says. "We believe its lower rate of growth also reflects the adoption of broader value seeking behavior across consumer goods." The company isn't sure yet if the company will revert to more normalized spending behaviors in the current quarter, the executive says. Shares fall 13% to $189.74. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

0924 ET - Gap's Athleta brand is just an average athletic apparel brand, UBS says in a research note. The company's Old Navy, Gap and Banana Republic brands are the ones within Gap's portfolio that will likely drive the stock price, according to the analysts. Athleta could become a 4th stock-driving brand for the company, but for now the analysts don't see it as one. UBS says its 10th annual global athletic wear survey suggests Athleta isn't a top-of-mind athletic wear brand in the U.S. "While the brand has room to improve, the survey suggests it lacks momentum with consumers." (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

0917 ET - European indexes fall after stronger-than-expected U.S. payroll data raised concerns that U.S. interest rates could stay higher for longer. Data showed the U.S. economy added 256,000 jobs in December. This, combined with expectations of an inflationary agenda under the incoming Trump administration, mean the Federal Reserve's December interest rate cut could be the last for some, head of fixed interest research at Quilter Cheviot Richard Carter writes. Spain's IBEX 35 falls 1.5%, Switzerland's SMI and Portugal's PSI 20 fall around 0.9% while the U.K.'s FTSE 100 is down 0.5%. The Netherlands AEX and France's CAC 40 both fall around 0.3%. The Euro Stoxx 50 is down 0.4% and the broader Stoxx Europe 600 is down 0.5%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0917 ET - Deutsche Bank expects SSAB to report a weak set of fourth quarter numbers, driven by weak demand and further price and margin pressure across all core units. It forecasts below-consensus Ebitda of 1.2 billion Swedish kronor and cuts its estimates by 1%-3% for 2024-2025. "We expect U.S. plate prices to remain in a tough spot as demand remains weak," analyst Bastian Synagowitz says. Deutsche Bank lowers its price target to 55 kronor from 58 kronor and keeps its hold rating. Shares trade 0.5% lower at 43.56 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0840 ET - Novo Nordisk is still a buy, but investors might need patience, Deutsche Bank analyst Emmanuel Papadakis writes. Novo Nordisk recently reported disappointing results of a closely watched clinical trial testing an experimental anti-obesity treatment. The bank lowers its share target price to 900 Danish kroner from 1,000 kroner to reflect the recent setback. It keeps its buy rating on the stock. Shares trade 0.7% higher at 634.60 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0824 ET - Politics weighs heavy on Vestas and the wind industry in general, with U.S. President-elect Trump signaling this week that he would oppose all new wind energy production, Deutsche Bank analyst John Kim writes. The U.S. remains strategic to Vestas and the industry as the largest market globally outside of China, Kim says. "We see offshore as more vulnerable than onshore due to a number of reasons with a potential repeal of relevant tax credits as another possible outcome." The bank's analysis suggests shares face 20% downside risk from a moratorium/freeze in the U.S. wind sector. It retains its buy rating and 145 Danish kroner target price, with most of the potential risk already priced in. Shares fall 0.6% to 96.24 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0752 ET - Renault stock is still rated as abuy, but the world is getting more complicated, Citi analysts Harald C Hendrikse and Soumava Banerjee write. Excluding Ferrari, Renault was the best performing stock in European autos in 2024. The French carmaker's management executed well and earnings were boosted by a disciplined sales channel strategy, new model roll-out with improved margins and a lack of China exposure. The company also benefited from much-improved free cash flow and balance sheet. However, risks from worsening macro and EU carbon dioxide regulations suggest the risk-reward has deteriorated, Citi says. Citi closes its short-term upside view and removes Renault from its focus list. It has a 52 euro target price on the stock. Shares trade 0.5% lower at 45.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0721 ET - The U.K. has less than a week of gas demand in store due to colder-than-usual weather in the country and the end of Russian gas pipeline supplies through Ukraine on December 31, 2024, Chris O'Shea, chief executive of Centrica, says. As of Thursday, U.K. storage sites were 26% lower than at the same time last year, leaving them around half full, the group says. O'Shea says the U.K. needs to boost its energy storage capacity as it becomes increasingly reliant on renewable sources. The U.K. has some of the lowest levels of gas storage in Europe at 12 days average or 7.5 peak winter days, compared to Germany at 89 days, and France at 103 days, the company notes. Centrica shares are up 0.45% at 1.33 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0709 ET - Delta doesn't see travel demand slowing down in 2025. CEO Ed Bastian notes consumers are increasingly opting for the airline's premium products and experiences, a phenomenon happening across the industry that more airlines are leaning into and prioritizing. A snapshot of this demand came in November and December when Delta saw four of the top 10 revenue days in its history and double-digit growth in cash bookings driven by both leisure and corporate travelers. President Glen Hauenstein says revenue growth in 4Q came in ahead of its guidance as demand trends accelerated through the quarter and expects adjusted revenue in the March quarter to be up to 9% higher than 2024. Delta climbs 5.3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Bitcoin may fall 20% to $73,000 if it breaks below this key support level
data.description:
By Frances Yue

This chart shows bitcoin is forming a top based on the so-called shoulder-head-shoulder pattern

Bitcoin may face a downside potential to $73,000 if it breaks below its support level at around $90,680, according to Kim Kramer Larsson, an analyst at Saxo Bank.

The largest cryptocurrency by market capitalization (BTCUSD) has fallen 3.9% over the past five days while it is up 2.6% on Friday to trade at around $94,061, according to the Dow Jones Market Data. The crypto is roughly 13.2% away from its record high at $108,309, reached on Dec. 17.

Bitcoin is forming a top based on the so-called shoulder-head-shoulder pattern, a chart formation in technical analysis that predicts a bullish-to-bearish trend reversal, Kramer Larsson wrote in a Friday post on X.

A head-and-shoulders pattern usually consists of three peaks, with the outer two close in height and the middle one being the highest.

If bitcoin breaks below its neckline support at around $90,680, it could potentially fall to $73,000 next, noted Kramer Larsson.

Some traders have been taking profits as bitcoin rose 120% in 2024.

Read: Higher bond yields could knock bitcoin back down to $90,000

-Frances Yue

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Constellation mega-deal delicately orbits AI sun
data.description:
The biggest US nuclear power producer agreed to buy gas-fueled rival Calpine for $27 bln to help feed insatiable high-tech demand. Optimistic projections have wrong-footed electricity providers before. In this case, though, the price is cheap and hedged by paying in stock.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow @rob_cyran on X

CONTEXT NEWS

Constellation Energy said on Jan. 10 it had agreed to buy Calpine for $16.4 billion of stock and cash and assume $12.7 billion of net debt as part of a deal that would combine two of the biggest U.S. power producers. After considering the cash to be generated between now and the transaction’s closing date, the total price will be $26.6 billion.

Calpine, which focuses on electricity powered by natural gas, is owned by Energy Capital Partners, Access Industries and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, which together bought the company in 2017 for $17 billion, including debt.

Constellation, the biggest U.S. nuclear power producer, was formed in 2022, after it was spun out of Exelon.

data.title: Nymex Overview: Oil, Products Jump on Sanction Worries, Bitter Cold — OPIS
data.description:
Crude oil futures rose about $2 around midday Friday as the possibility of more U.S. sanctions against Russian petroleum products triggered global supply worries.

Diesel contracts also jumped as below-average temperatures in a large portion of the U.S. driven by a polar vortex sparked heating oil demand.

At 11:45 a.m. ET, February Nymex West Texas Intermediate crude futures rose $1.80 to around $75.70/bbl and March WTI was $1.65 higher to $74.90/bbl.

London-based March Brent crude increased $1.85 to $78.80/bbl and April Brent gained $1.70 to $77.95/bbl.

For the week, both WTI and Brent are on track to rack up gains of about $2.

According to a Reuters report, the U.S. will impose some of its harshest sanctions on the Russian oil industry, designating 180 vessels, traders and two major oil companies as well as top Russian oil executives.

Nymex February ULSD futures jumped 8.3cts to $2.461/gal and March ULSD climbed 8.05cts to $2.4385/gal. February RBOB was up 3cts at $2.0586/gal and March RBOB increased 3.05cts to around $2.087/gal.

A large swath of the U.S. is currently under a prolonged cold spell that is expected to last until late January. Parts of Texas and the South are also being battered by unusual winter storms which should sharply increase distillate demand as a residential and commercial heating fuel.

Surging petroleum futures on Friday came against the backdrop of a sharply higher dollar and weakness in the equity markets. A strong December U.S. jobs report released earlier Friday smashed investor hopes that the Federal Reserve would further cut interest rates to stimulate the economy.

data.title: TSX Down 350 Points With Energy The Sole Gainer
data.description:
The Toronto Stock Exchange is sharply lower in Friday midday trading, down 350 points with most sectors in the red.

The biggest decliners are health care (-4.2%) and utilities (-2%) and financials (-2%).

Energy is the sole gainer, up 1.3%.

Canada added 91K jobs in December (56k in fulltime), surprising economists who had forecasted a 20K gain that would leave the unemployment rate unchanged or worse case scenario see a slight bump. Public sector rose 40K in December with the private sector accounting for 27K.

Meanwhile, "The US labor market is healthy," said CIBC's Ali Jaffery after U.S. payrolls came in at 256K, "smashing expectations" of 165K by consensus and above last month's 212K gain. Jaffery noted there were only 8K net negative revisions over the prior two months, and the three-month average job gain sits at 170K as of December, about 10-20K higher than where it was in the middle of 2024.

"The Fed has been less focused on the job market recently and today's reason gives them more reason to put their attention elsewhere. The labor market has been volatile to close out 2024, but the latest data makes it clear that the trend in cyclical employment is solid. The FOMC is more concerned about seeing inflation progress being sustained, and seeing where trade policy actually goes. We expect they will be on hold this month," Jaffery said.

In stock specific news, Apollo Global Management Inc. is considering taking a substantial stake in a bid by Seven & i's founding Ito family to take the Japanese convenience store operator private, Bloomberg is reporting, citing people familiar with the matter. The US private equity giant is discussing a commitment of as much as (Yen)1.5 trillion (US$9.5 billion) for an equity stake in the plan, said the sources. who requested anonymity, as the information isn't public. This trumps the (Yen)7.5 trillion takeover bid from Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc. , but may be lowered as the Japanese company's market value on Friday hovered around (Yen)6.3 trillion after stocks dipped over third quarter earnings.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0626 ET - UBS sees slowing service revenues and equity free cash flow consensus cuts at Tele2. The bank understands the midterm appeal, but says peers offer better momentum and value. Longer term, potential attractions include market exposure, dividend policy, capital expenditure normalization and potential consolidation in Sweden, among other things. In the near-term, however, UBS thinks Tele2 has weakening momentum, and looks unlikely to cover its dividend. "We believe the next few quarters will likely be negative for Tele2." A derating and consensus downgrades are likely before the mid-term story becomes more convincing, it says. UBS downgrades Tele2 to sell from neutral and lowers its price target to 102 Swedish kronor from 104 kronor. Shares fall 3% to 106.50 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0528 ET - Deutsche Telekom is well-placed for sustained growth in the U.S., UBS analysts write in a research note. "While German mobile competition is increasing, the bulk of DT's German revenue are fixed-line and investors may be more focused on the upside from U.S. domestic exposure post the outcome of the presidential elections," the analysts say. The U.S. market is healthy, facing less price competition than many European markets, they add. The U.S. bank raises its recommendation on the stock to buy from neutral and its target price to 33 euros from 31 euros. Shares are up 1.5% at 29.70 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0032 ET - Tencent's overall business fundamentals and growth outlook remain intact despite recently being added to a Pentagon list with alleged Chinese military ties, OCBC analysts write in a note. However, the news may indicate rising geopolitical tensions and could be a share-price overhang until further clarity on the development, they say. Tencent's key businesses are value-added services and online advertising, which remain positive over the long term. The recent share-price selloff may be an overreaction and provides for a long-term accumulation opportunity, they say. OCBC maintains a buy rating on the stock at fair value of HK$560.00. Shares are last at HK$373.00. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2319 ET - Xiaomi's core businesses likely remained resilient in 4Q of 2024, OCBC analysts write in a note. The company was a "star performer" last year as share prices more than doubled on year, they say. The strong share performance was driven by solid growth in its core businesses on resilient margins and stronger-than-expected EV business margins, they say. Xiaomi's smartphone segment gross profit margin may bottom out in 4Q with potential improvement ahead given higher average selling price and lower component cost, they add. OCBC maintains its buy rating for the stock with a fair value at HK$40.50. Shares last at HK$33.60.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2219 ET - Thailand's telecom sector is likely to face some headwinds in 2025-2026, CGS International's Weerapat Wonk-Urai says in a research report as the brokerage downgrades the sector to neutral from overweight. Headwinds include slower organic growth and potentially higher regulatory risk owing to upcoming spectrum auction, the analyst says. Also, the sector's valuation of 3.4X estimated 2025 price-to-book-value ratio, which is two standard deviations above its six-year historical average, looks expensive to CGS. The brokerage maintains hold ratings and target prices of THB287.00 and THB12.70 for Advanced Info Service and True Corp., respectively. Advanced Info Service shares are 0.4% higher at THB277.00; True Corp. shares are 0.9% lower at THB10.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2020 ET - The U.S.'s planned restrictions on artificial intelligence chip exports are expected to have limited direct impact on Malaysian technology companies, given their minimal role in the global AI supply chain, RHB IB analyst Lee Meng Horng says in a note. While indirect effects are harder to gauge, the local tech sector's exposure remains low, he reckons. Lee remains optimistic about a stronger 2025, driven by a sector recovery supported by robust broad-based demand and replacement cycle. RHB maintains an overweight rating on the Malaysian tech sector and pegs Malaysian Pacific Industries, CTOS Digital, Coraza Integrated Technology and Datasonic Group as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1223 ET - The U.K.'s Competition watchdog underestimates the havoc that could be wreaked by letting browsers that rival Safari operate on iPhones and iPads without relying on Apple's WebKit engine, Apple says in response to the regulator's investigation into mobile browsers and cloud gaming. "The importance of mandating a high base level of stability, security, and privacy should not be understated," the iPhone maker says. A panel of experts told the CMA in November it should consider investigating Apple and Google's business practices under the U.K.'s new digital competition rules. Alphabet's Google also says in response that the watchdog's findings don't account for evidence that shows there is strong browser competition on Android devices. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0840 ET - Novo Nordisk is still a buy, but investors might need patience, Deutsche Bank analyst Emmanuel Papadakis writes. Novo Nordisk recently reported disappointing results of a closely watched clinical trial testing an experimental anti-obesity treatment. The bank lowers its share target price to 900 Danish kroner from 1,000 kroner to reflect the recent setback. It keeps its buy rating on the stock. Shares trade 0.7% higher at 634.60 kroner. 0045 ET - Zydus Lifesciences' earnings are poised to rise on higher sales contribution from Sitagliptin 505b(2) drug, Nomura analysts say in a research report. Assuming Sitagliptin market at $300-$500 million at a discounted price, the opportunity size for Zydus Lifesciences could be as big as $100-$150 million annualized, the analysts say. 1421 ET - Biotech's year ahead looks uncertain, but it could still be a safer option in a market downturn, Baird analysts say. The unknowns of a Trump administration, including possible tariffs and regulatory changes, make it hard to predict industry growth, though analysts are cautiously optimistic. If inflation and volatility worsen, analysts believe it could revamp investor confidence in large-cap biotech companies as a safe place to keep their money. data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1130 ET - Constellation Brands' marketing efforts put in place for its beer business in the second quarter to offset a softer macroeconomic backdrop that began affecting consumer spending during this summer bear fruit in the third quarter, CEO Bill Newlands says on a call with analysts. Still, beverage alcohol remains relatively unchanged in its share of total consumer expenditures, Newlands says. "We believe its lower rate of growth also reflects the adoption of broader value seeking behavior across consumer goods." The company isn't sure yet if the company will revert to more normalized spending behaviors in the current quarter, the executive says. Shares fall 13% to $189.74. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

0939 ET - Bitcoin stands to benefit from improved risk sentiment after Friday's stronger-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payrolls report, 21Shares crypto strategist Matt Mena says in a note. The data highlights the resilience of the labor market, alleviating fears of an imminent recession and boosting risk sentiment, he says. "This favorable economic backdrop serves as a significant tailwind for bitcoin, which tends to thrive in an environment of growing investor confidence." The data could provide the momentum needed for bitcoin to return above $100,000 again and surge past last year's record high. Bitcoin rises 2.4% to $94,347, according to LSEG. It reached a record high of $108,379 on Dec. 17. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0221 ET - BBVA's reduction of the acceptance level of its $12 billion-plus hostile bid to take over Banco de Sabadell is mostly a technical adjustment at the request of Spanish regulators, RBC Capital Markets' Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say in a research note. BBVA said its offer requires more than half of the effective voting rights of Sabadell, excluding treasury shares. Regulators saw a need to clarify what BBVA's acceptance threshold was and the Spanish bank's move effectively means the minimum acceptance is reduced by 0.7%, according to RBC. "We continue to be of the view that this deal gets done, probably by [the first half], and probably with some relatively minor remedies and a 10% increase in the offer price," the analysts say. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

2138 ET - Current market expectations and trading multiples have set a very high bar for Australia's banks in 2025, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. "While multiples have remained elevated for many months, we think a de-rating is likely in the year ahead," they say. As share prices stand, meaningful upgrades to consensus EPS and dividend forecasts appear to already be priced in. "There is very little margin for error," say the analysts. They prefer nonbank financial stocks in Australia this year. Among major Australian banks, MS's order of preference is overweight-rated NAB, followed by equal-weight-rated ANZ, and then underweight-rated Westpac and CBA. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2020 ET - The U.S.'s planned restrictions on artificial intelligence chip exports are expected to have limited direct impact on Malaysian technology companies, given their minimal role in the global AI supply chain, RHB IB analyst Lee Meng Horng says in a note. While indirect effects are harder to gauge, the local tech sector's exposure remains low, he reckons. Lee remains optimistic about a stronger 2025, driven by a sector recovery supported by robust broad-based demand and replacement cycle. RHB maintains an overweight rating on the Malaysian tech sector and pegs Malaysian Pacific Industries, CTOS Digital, Coraza Integrated Technology and Datasonic Group as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1103 ET - Crude futures test new multi-month highs after a Reuters report that the U.S. plans to expand sanctions on Russian oil. "I think these reports are being taken more seriously now because we're starting to see China and India back away from actually buying the sanctioned barrels," says Rebecca Babin of CIBC Private Wealth US. Previous buyer reaction to increased sanctions was "yeah, whatever, you're not going to enforce, now they're taking it a bit more seriously," she adds. The other factor pushing oil higher is winter weather driving up heating oil demand. WTI is up 3.2% at $76.27 a barrel, and Brent is 3% higher at $79.23 after touching highs of $77.86 and $80.75, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0807 ET - The winter weather that has lifted natural gas prices is extending to oil as concerns about supply from Russia and Iran coincide with higher demand for heating fuels. "Weather roulette has hit the oil patch this morning," with American and European models showing extreme cold for Texas and raising fears of freeze-offs, TP ICAP's Scott Shelton says in a note. While ensemble runs aren't as cold, "the risks are clearly there for Texas with possibly some refinery freezing issues as well," he adds. WTI is up 3.5% at $76.48 a barrel, and Brent is 3.5% higher at $79.62 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0721 ET - The U.K. has less than a week of gas demand in store due to colder-than-usual weather in the country and the end of Russian gas pipeline supplies through Ukraine on December 31, 2024, Chris O'Shea, chief executive of Centrica, says. As of Thursday, U.K. storage sites were 26% lower than at the same time last year, leaving them around half full, the group says. O'Shea says the U.K. needs to boost its energy storage capacity as it becomes increasingly reliant on renewable sources. The U.K. has some of the lowest levels of gas storage in Europe at 12 days average or 7.5 peak winter days, compared to Germany at 89 days, and France at 103 days, the company notes. Centrica shares are up 0.45% at 1.33 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

2118 ET - The progress of Dialog Group's Baram Junior Cluster field development is progressing positively and could strengthen the company's domestic upstream portfolio, RHB IB analyst Sean Lim says in a note. The project recently received a final investment decision, with an estimated capital expenditure of US$235 million. Following the approval, Dialog is set to begin a two-year development phase, Lim notes. The project is targeting first gas production by end-2026, and could contribute approximately MYR40 million annually to Dialog's earnings, assuming a project internal rate of return of 12%, he adds. RHB raises Dialog's target price to MYR3.11 from MYR3.09, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.5% lower at MYR1.83. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1654 ET - An artificial intelligence-fueled boom of data-center construction is lifting demand for electricity and creating opportunities for developers of tools that can help make power systems more resilient to weather-related disruptions, says Jay Koh, a managing director at Lightsmith Group in New York. The private-equity firm last year invested in artificial-intelligence startup AiDash, whose software helps utilities keep trees away from power lines to reduce wildfire risks. "As you see increasing volatility due to extreme weather events, whether it's fire risk or storm risk or flood risk or heat waves, we think that there's going to be enormous amounts of demand for resilience of the energy system," Koh says. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1648 ET - Australian stocks are expected to open higher, with ASX futures up 0.3% before the bell. Australia's benchmark index lacks typical cues from Wall Street, where U.S. stock markets were closed for the day to mark former President Jimmy Carter's memorial. The S&P/ASX 200 edged 0.2% lower Thursday, snapping the five-day winning streak it recorded to start the new year. Firmer commodity prices may be a tailwind for some mining and energy stocks, with iron ore, gold, copper and crude oil all gaining. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1601 ET - Oil futures settle higher amid signs of strong demand, including seven straight weeks of U.S. inventory draws and prospects of higher heating fuel use. Although last week's 1 million barrel crude storage decline reported by the EIA was smaller than the API's 4 million barrel estimate, "it still was a decline," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "The negatives that remain are builds in storage for gasoline and diesel inventories along with a very strong U.S. dollar index," he says. February WTI remains technically bullish above key moving averages, with resistance around $75.29-$76.41, Kissler adds. WTI settles up 0.8% at $73.92 a barrel, and Brent gains 1% to $76.92 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1107 ET - Gold futures jumped after the release of the jobs report--which showed 256,000 jobs being added in December, more than expected by the market. "Gold's strength following a robust jobs report might seem counterintuitive, as it typically suggests a stronger dollar and higher yields, which are headwinds for gold," says Alex Ebkarian of Allegiance Gold. "But this reaction highlights a broader unease among investors--it reflects deeper fears about structural issues like unsustainable debt levels, overvalued markets, and geopolitical uncertainty." Gold is now up 1.5%, to $2,732 an ounce--nearing the record of $2,800 an ounce set in October. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

It forecasts below-consensus Ebitda of 1.2 billion Swedish kronor and cuts its estimates by 1%-3% for 2024-2025. "We expect U.S. plate prices to remain in a tough spot as demand remains weak," analyst Bastian Synagowitz says. Deutsche Bank lowers its price target to 55 kronor from 58 kronor and keeps its hold rating. Shares trade 0.5% lower at 43.56 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

RHB raises Dialog's target price to MYR3.11 from MYR3.09, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.5% lower at MYR1.83. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1957 ET - Posco Holdings could post below-consensus 4Q earnings on narrower profit margins, Daishin Securities analyst Lee Tae-hwan writes in a note. The South Korean steelmaker may have sequentially recovered in sales, which likely topped 8.4 million metric tons of steel in volume for the October-December period. However, its profit margins may have weakened due to lower steel-product prices, Lee says. He estimates Posco's 4Q operating profit at KRW474 billion, well below the market consensus forecast of KRW698 billion. Daishin cuts its target price for the stock by 13% to KRW410,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 1.0% lower at KRW258,500. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1648 ET - Australian stocks are expected to open higher, with ASX futures up 0.3% before the bell. Australia's benchmark index lacks typical cues from Wall Street, where U.S. stock markets were closed for the day to mark former President Jimmy Carter's memorial. The S&P/ASX 200 edged 0.2% lower Thursday, snapping the five-day winning streak it recorded to start the new year. Firmer commodity prices may be a tailwind for some mining and energy stocks, with iron ore, gold, copper and crude oil all gaining. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1438 ET - Front-month gold futures close up 0.7% to $2,683.80 an ounce. It's the third day in a row that gold moved higher, this despite indications from the Federal Reserve that only one rate cut may come in 2025, which the market takes as a negative for assets like gold. "Fed minutes on Wednesday said that 'almost all participants judged that upside risks to the inflation outlook had increased,'" says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA in a note. Gold managed to shake this off, as did most commodities with the exception of some agricultural and soft commodities. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1252 GMT - Renault stock is still rated as abuy, but the world is getting more complicated, Citi analysts Harald C Hendrikse and Soumava Banerjee write. Excluding Ferrari, Renault was the best performing stock in European autos in 2024. The French carmaker's management executed well and earnings were boosted by a disciplined sales channel strategy, new model roll-out with improved margins and a lack of China exposure. The company also benefited from much-improved free cash flow and balance sheet. However, risks from worsening macro and EU carbon dioxide regulations suggest the risk-reward has deteriorated, Citi says. Citi closes its short-term upside view and removes Renault from its focus list. It has a 52 euro target price on the stock. Shares trade 0.5% lower at 45.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1109 GMT - Delta doesn't see travel demand slowing down in 2025. CEO Ed Bastian notes consumers are increasingly opting for the airline's premium products and experiences, a phenomenon happening across the industry that more airlines are leaning into and prioritizing. A snapshot of this demand came in November and December when Delta saw four of the top 10 revenue days in its history and double-digit growth in cash bookings driven by both leisure and corporate travelers. President Glen Hauenstein says revenue growth in 4Q came in ahead of its guidance as demand trends accelerated through the quarter and expects adjusted revenue in the March quarter to be up to 9% higher than 2024. Delta climbs 5.3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1203 GMT - Stellantis shares are cheap, but 2025 remains uncertain, Citi analysts Harald C Hendrikse and Soumava Banerjee write. Although the company isn't Citi's favorite pick, it understands why investors may look at it again. Stellantis was the worst performer in European autos last year, but the company's U.S. exposure versus no China exposure, continued strong U.S. market demand and weak sentiment could see the shares trade higher in early 2025 as investors hope for a recovery. "We still prefer other names on lower valuation (Renault, Volkswagen), and Porsche on earnings stabilization." It raises its target price to 13 euros from 12.40 euros, opens a 90-day positive short-term view, and keeps a neutral rating. Shares rise 1.4% to 12.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1147 GMT - The U.K. Competition and Markets Authority's investigation into Global Business Travel's planned $570 million bid for CWT Holdings is fatally flawed, a filing from both companies says. A CMA probe found in November that the two companies compete closely for multinational customers and their merger risks reducing choice and raising prices. The companies hit back saying the CMA's definition of their market was "fundamentally erroneous". They said the regulator's interim decision was "fatally undermined by a biased and unrepresentative market investigation," and insisted they faced strong competition from other travel companies as well as hotels and airlines. GBT and CWT offered concessions, including price commitments and selling off some customer contracts to a rival, to get their deal greenlit by CMA's provisional Jan. 26 deadline and to end the probe. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - ComfortDelGro faces increased competition in 2025, analysts at UOB Kay Hian write in a note. The Singapore-based company raises its taxi platform fees to offset a new industry-wide rule requiring higher contributions to platform workers' state-run pension plans. Meanwhile, two new competitors enter the ride-hailing market, further intensifying competition, the analysts note. A bus contract currently operated by ComfortDelGro's subsidiary, SBS Transit, is up for tender. This poses a near-term earnings risk due to competitive bidding, which is likely to result in lower margins, they add. UOB Kay Hian reduces its 2025-2026 profit after tax and minority interests estimates by 2%-3%. While maintaining a buy rating, the brokerate trims its target price to S$1.77 from S$1.83. Shares are flat at S$1.44. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)
